metadata.description: Agent skills written by Gabriel Bauman and Claude

plugins.name: adr-workflow
plugins.description: ADR-driven development: atomic, immutable Architecture Decision Records as the sole behavioural spec, strict spec/code commit separation, inline ADR tags in code, and git-hook enforcement tooling

plugins.name: api-design
plugins.description: Design and evolve APIs without breaking deployed consumers: what counts as a breaking change, additive evolution with deprecate-then-remove, versioning as a last resort, and consumer-first endpoint design with pagination and an error contract from day one

plugins.name: atomic-commits
plugins.description: Decide what each git commit should contain: one self-contained, reviewable change per commit, splitting a mixed working tree into an ordered series with git add -p instead of one everything-commit

plugins.name: branch-review
plugins.description: Pre-merge branch review with parallel specialist agents and an adversarial challenge round: charter-scoped reviewers find issues, a verifying challenger dismisses false positives, and review depth scales with the branch so small diffs skip the fleet

plugins.name: bug-reports
plugins.description: Write bug reports a stranger can act on: one bug per report, symptom-plus-condition titles, expected vs actual, numbered repro steps from a clean start, exact error text pasted verbatim, environment and frequency, and speculation labeled separately from observation

plugins.name: changelog
plugins.description: Write CHANGELOG.md entries by curating the diff for end users instead of transcribing the git log: Common Changelog format, breaking changes bolded first with migration pointers, and rules for what deserves no entry at all

plugins.name: db-migrations
plugins.description: Change database schemas without breaking the running system: expand and contract instead of in-place renames, backfills as batched resumable jobs separate from DDL, destructive steps in their own later deploy, applied migrations never edited, and lock behavior checked before DDL on hot tables

plugins.name: debugging
plugins.description: Debug systematically instead of speculatively: reproduce the failure first, read the actual error instead of pattern-matching to a famous fix, test one hypothesis at a time, isolate before changing code, fix the cause rather than the nearest symptom, and verify against the original reproduction

plugins.name: dependencies
plugins.description: Manage third-party dependencies as long-term liabilities: vet a package before adding it (maintenance, transitive weight, license, exact name), commit the lockfile, bump one dependency at a time with the upstream changelog read first, keep bumps out of feature branches, and remove packages that stop paying rent

plugins.name: error-handling
plugins.description: Handle errors so failures are loud, actionable, and traceable: fail early instead of limping on bad state, never swallow an exception silently, write messages that name what was attempted with which inputs, preserve the underlying cause when rethrowing, catch at the boundary that can act, choose retry vs propagate vs crash, and never leak secrets or internals to users

plugins.name: flaky-tests
plugins.description: Treat intermittently failing tests as bugs to fix, not nuisances to rerun: quarantine visibly with an owner and expiry, reproduce with a counted loop, diagnose the usual suspects (sleeps, test order, shared state, randomness, clocks, network, leaked resources), and fix the mechanism instead of widening the timeout

plugins.name: git-recovery
plugins.description: Reshape and recover git history safely: interactive rebase to reorder, squash, split, and reword; fixup plus autosquash to amend an earlier commit; reset --soft/--mixed/--hard and when each is right; --force-with-lease over --force; the reflog as the undo of last resort; git revert for shared commits; and the non-interactive rebase alternatives for agent environments

plugins.name: pr-description
plugins.description: Write the PR description for the reviewer, not the machine: lead with what changed and why, give a reading order and the parts to scrutinize, flag the risky hunk, state what was left out of scope, and link the issue instead of retyping it

plugins.name: humanize
plugins.description: Write prose that reads like a person wrote it and strip AI tells from existing text: em-dash addiction, negative parallelism, inflated vocabulary, bold-first bullets, manufactured drama

plugins.name: logging
plugins.description: Write logs an operator can act on: match the level to the reader and the action (ERROR someone must act, WARN handled, INFO milestone, DEBUG diagnostic), prefer structured key-value fields over interpolated strings so logs are queryable, log the ids and inputs and attempt needed to act instead of a bare 'failed', never log secrets or PII, log at boundaries and state transitions not every line, and do not log-and-rethrow the same error at every layer

plugins.name: naming
plugins.description: Name variables, functions, types, and files for intent and role, not implementation or type: replace vague fillers (data, temp, handle), drop noise words (Manager, Helper, Util), strip type-restating and Hungarian suffixes, make booleans read as predicates, scale name length to scope, and match the codebase's conventions

plugins.name: performance
plugins.description: Optimize measured hotspots against a stated target instead of guessing: profile first, set a numeric goal so you know when to stop, take structural wins (N+1 queries, missing indexes, quadratic loops) before micro-optimizations, change one thing at a time and re-measure, treat caching as a last resort, and record before/after numbers

plugins.name: reading-code
plugins.description: Understand unfamiliar code before changing it: orient from entry points, trace real call paths instead of trusting grep hits, read tests as the intent spec, recover intent from git history, find the codebase's existing way first, and verify your model before editing on it

plugins.name: refactoring
plugins.description: Refactor safely: change structure while preserving observable behavior exactly, keep the tests green after every step, work in small reversible moves instead of a big-bang rewrite, add characterization tests before touching untested code, and commit the refactor apart from any behavior change

plugins.name: reviewing-code
plugins.description: Review pull requests like a good colleague: understand the intent before judging the diff, separate blocking defects from suggestions and nits, ground every blocker in a defect or written rule rather than taste, ask genuine questions, leave style to the tools, stay inside the PR's scope, and end with an explicit verdict

plugins.name: scoped-commits
plugins.description: Write git commit messages in the Scoped Commits style (scope: description), with a concise plain-ASCII house style: no emoji, no em dashes, no filler

plugins.name: secure-coding
plugins.description: Write code that holds at the trust boundary: treat all external input as attacker-controlled, parameterize instead of concatenating SQL, shell, HTML, and paths, authorize at every resource access, keep secrets out of code, logs, and URLs, use vetted crypto and password hashing, and fail closed when checks cannot complete

plugins.name: small-prs
plugins.description: Keep each branch or PR down to one reviewable idea: state a scope contract before coding, park drive-by fixes instead of folding them in, and split big asks into a planned series of self-contained steps, refactor first

plugins.name: test-behavior
plugins.description: Write tests that verify observable behavior instead of pinning the implementation: assert on outcomes rather than mock calls and call counts, mock only at boundaries you do not control, name tests as behavior claims, cover boundaries and error paths, and reproduce bugs in a failing test before fixing

plugins.name: why-comments
plugins.description: Write code comments that explain why, never what: comment only on constraints, invariants, workarounds, and rejected alternatives, strip narration noise, keep docstrings as contracts, and enforce TODO hygiene

plugins.name: writing-docs
plugins.description: Write documentation for the reader arriving with a task: lead the README with a copy-pasteable quickstart, keep tutorials, how-tos, reference, and explanation separate, prefer runnable examples over prose, document the sharp edges users actually hit, and fix or delete stale docs on sight
